med_suggestions.notes: Pregnancy-compatible option; a safe default when pregnancy is possible/confirmed.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Cephalosporin allergy; use caution with immediate-type severe beta-lactam allergy; adjust if renal impairment.
Immediate-type beta-lactam allergy; adjust for renal impairment.

med_suggestions.notes: Common first-line for uncomplicated cystitis when not near term and renal function adequate.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Avoid if pyelonephritis suspected (fever, flank pain); avoid if CrCl < 30 mL/min; nitrofurantoin allergy; caution near term pregnancy.

med_suggestions.notes: Single-dose alternative; generally pregnancy-compatible but confirm clinician judgment and local guidance.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Not for suspected pyelonephritis; consider availability/cost.

med_suggestions.dosing: 160/800 mg (1 DS tab) PO BID x 3 days
med_suggestions.sig: 160/800 mg (1 DS tab) PO BID x 3 days
med_suggestions.notes: Alternative when pregnancy status/trimester allows and resistance risk acceptable.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Sulfa allergy; avoid 1st trimester if possible (folate antagonist) and near term (kernicterus risk); consider local resistance; drug interactions.

note.soap:
SOAP Note:

S: 
- Patient is a female aged 18-64 years.
- Chief complaint: burning sensation when urinating.
- Duration of symptoms: 4 days.
- No fever reported.
- No flank pain.
- No vaginal discharge.
- Patient reports history of renal disease.
- Pregnancy is denied.

O: 
- No physical exam findings available due to phone-only encounter.
- No vital signs available.

A: 
- Likely uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection (cystitis) given dysuria without fever, flank pain, or discharge.
- History of renal disease noted, which may influence medication choice.
- No signs of upper urinary tract involvement or systemic infection.

P: 
  Medication suggestions filtered due to contraindication flags (renal disease / reduced kidney function, beta-lactam allergy (penicillin/PCN)). Physician review required.
  For physician review: consider fosfomycin 3 g PO once for uncomplicated cystitis if clinically appropriate.
- Recommend empiric antibiotic therapy for uncomplicated UTI, pending clinician review to select appropriate agent and dosing considering renal disease.
- OTC options for symptom relief: acetaminophen if no contraindications.
- Avoid NSAIDs due to renal disease.
- Phenazopyridine may be considered for urinary burning relief; confirm no pregnancy (patient denies pregnancy).
- Advise patient to seek urgent care if fever, flank pain, worsening symptoms, or other red flags develop.
- Follow-up as needed after antibiotic initiation or if symptoms persist beyond expected course.
note.patient_summary: It sounds like you’re experiencing a likely urinary tract infection, which is causing the burning when you urinate. Because you have a history of kidney issues, a licensed physician will review your information to decide if medication is appropriate and which one is safest for you. For now, you can take acetaminophen (Tylenol) to help with any discomfort, but avoid ibuprofen or other pain relievers that might affect your kidneys. If your symptoms get worse, like developing a fever, pain in your side, or if the burning worsens, please seek in-person care the same day at an urgent care or emergency room. A doctor will review your case soon and may prescribe treatment if needed. If symptoms don’t improve after starting medication or you have concerns, be sure to get evaluated in person.
patient_instructions: If symptoms worsen or you develop new severe symptoms, seek urgent care or call 911.
Follow the disposition and safety net plan explained in the spoken summary.

Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely (no dosing provided); ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely (no dosing provided); phenazopyridine (AZO) may help urinary burning; turns urine orange; does not treat infection (no dosing provided)

Medication cautions: Avoid NSAIDs if kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding history, or on blood thinners (reviewer to confirm).; Avoid phenazopyridine if pregnant; reviewer to confirm pregnancy status.
clinician_questions: Rx candidates for clinician review: If uncomplicated UTI likely and no red flags: consider empiric antibiotic per guideline (reviewer selects agent/dose/duration).
Reviewer check: Confirm pregnancy status; if pregnant or possibly pregnant, tailor evaluation/treatment.
Reviewer check: Any flank pain/fever suggesting pyelonephritis (would need urgent in-person evaluation)?
Reviewer check: Any male patient, immunocompromised, or recurrent UTI (may need different workup)?
